skill.name: reading-logseq-data
skill.description: Read Logseq graph data through the local HTTP API or Logseq CLI. Fetch pages, blocks, properties, backlinks, searches, and Datalog query results.
skill.summary: Read pages, blocks, properties, backlinks, and Datalog query results from Logseq graphs.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: AI review confirms legitimate but high-risk behavior: the skill ships a Python client that can call a token-authenticated Logseq HTTP API and execute the local Logseq CLI. Most Markdown backtick, weak crypto, and reconnaissance static hits are false positives, and no prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration intent was found.

high_findings.title: Bearer Token Sent to Configurable HTTP Endpoint
high_findings.description: The client reads LOGSEQ_API_URL and LOGSEQ_API_TOKEN, then sends the token in an Authorization header to the configured URL. This is intended for a local Logseq API, but a changed URL or config can disclose the token to another endpoint.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network call and bearer token header are explicit, and the URL can come from environment or config. The intended local Logseq use is legitimate, so this is high risk rather than confirmed malicious.

high_findings.title: External Logseq CLI Execution with Sensitive Arguments
high_findings.description: The client executes the logseq binary with user-provided Datalog text and may pass an API token on the command line. Argument-list subprocess use reduces shell injection risk, but it still executes a local command and can expose graph contents or token material.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct and semantically required by the skill. They are not shell=True, but command execution and command-line token handling are real publication risks.

medium_findings.title: Unescaped Search Text Interpolated into Datalog Query
medium_findings.description: The HTTP search fallback inserts query_text directly into a Datalog string. A crafted search term could change query semantics or cause unexpected graph reads when the fallback path is used.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The string interpolation is explicit and uses caller-controlled text inside a query. The impact is limited to Logseq query behavior, so the finding is medium severity.

medium_findings.title: Raw Datalog Query Capability Can Read Broad Graph Data
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally exposes datalog_query and templates using pull all attributes. This is useful, but users may unintentionally request broad reads of private notes, properties, and relationships.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The capability is explicit and central to the skill. It is legitimate but increases privacy risk because Logseq graphs often contain personal data.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many static external command hits in SKILL.md and references/read-operations.md are Markdown inline code or fenced examples. They document Python and Datalog usage and are not Ruby backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visibly Markdown tables and examples. No Ruby execution context exists in those files.

low_findings.title: Crypto and Reconnaissance Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak crypto and reconnaissance hits appear to match words such as MD, UUID, graph info, count, and recursive query examples. No evidence found of cryptographic operations, host scanning, or system inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain Logseq documentation and Datalog examples, not crypto or system reconnaissance code. This supports dismissing those static findings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token Sent to Configurable HTTP Endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: The client reads LOGSEQ_API_URL and LOGSEQ_API_TOKEN, then sends the token in an Authorization header to the configured URL. This is intended for a local Logseq API, but a changed URL or config can disclose the token to another endpoint.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network call and bearer token header are explicit, and the URL can come from environment or config. The intended local Logseq use is legitimate, so this is high risk rather than confirmed malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Logseq CLI Execution with Sensitive Arguments
dangerous_patterns.description: The client executes the logseq binary with user-provided Datalog text and may pass an API token on the command line. Argument-list subprocess use reduces shell injection risk, but it still executes a local command and can expose graph contents or token material.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct and semantically required by the skill. They are not shell=True, but command execution and command-line token handling are real publication risks.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unescaped Search Text Interpolated into Datalog Query
dangerous_patterns.description: The HTTP search fallback inserts query_text directly into a Datalog string. A crafted search term could change query semantics or cause unexpected graph reads when the fallback path is used.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The string interpolation is explicit and uses caller-controlled text inside a query. The impact is limited to Logseq query behavior, so the finding is medium severity.

content.value_statement: Logseq users need reliable ways to inspect pages, blocks, properties, and backlinks without manual copying. This skill provides reusable HTTP, CLI, and Datalog patterns for local graph reads.
actual_capabilities: Fetch Logseq pages by title, with optional child block retrieval.
Fetch individual blocks by UUID and include child blocks when requested.
Run Datalog queries against a Logseq graph through HTTP or CLI backends.
Search graph content and return matching blocks with page context.
List pages, inspect page or block properties, and retrieve backlinks.
Load Logseq API URL, token, and graph path from environment or local config.
limitations: Requires a running Logseq HTTP API or an installed Logseq CLI.
Needs a valid Logseq API token for HTTP access.
Does not write, create, delete, or modify Logseq data.
Raw Datalog output shape depends on the query and graph schema.

use_cases.title: Review Project Notes
use_cases.description: Fetch project pages, open related blocks, and inspect backlinks before planning work.

use_cases.title: Analyze Personal Knowledge Graphs
use_cases.description: Run Datalog queries to find tagged notes, properties, tasks, and relationships.

use_cases.title: Build Logseq Automations
use_cases.description: Use the Python client as a reusable read layer for local reporting workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Get a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch the Logseq page named "Project Alpha" and summarize its properties and top-level blocks.

prompt_templates.title: Find Backlinks
prompt_templates.prompt: Find all backlinks to "Project Alpha" in Logseq and group them by source page.

prompt_templates.title: Search by Topic
prompt_templates.prompt: Search my Logseq graph for "meeting notes" and list the most relevant matching blocks with page titles.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Focused Datalog Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a Datalog query that finds tasks with status "In Progress" and returns their titles and deadlines.

output_examples.input: Fetch the page "Reading List" and show its properties.
output_examples.output: The response lists the page title, available properties such as author and rating, and a short summary of included blocks.

output: A grouped list of pages that reference the target page.
Each item includes the referencing page title and a short block excerpt.

output_examples.output: The response provides matching block excerpts, their parent page names, and identifiers when available.

best_practices: Use the local Logseq API URL and avoid sending tokens to remote endpoints.
Prefer parameterized Datalog queries when accepting user-provided values.
Limit broad pull queries to the fields needed for the current task.
anti_patterns: Do not run broad graph queries when a page, block, or property lookup is enough.
Do not store Logseq API tokens in shared prompts, logs, or command history.
Do not assume every graph uses the same custom property names or classes.

faq.question: Does this skill modify Logseq data?
faq.answer: No. The documented operations focus on reading pages, blocks, properties, searches, backlinks, and query results.

faq.question: What access does it need?
faq.answer: It needs a local Logseq HTTP API token or access to the Logseq CLI and graph path.

faq.question: Can it run custom Datalog queries?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a datalog_query operation and templates for common Logseq query patterns.

faq.question: Can it search across my graph?
faq.answer: Yes. It can use Logseq search through the API or a Datalog fallback query.

faq.question: Does it work without the HTTP API?
faq.answer: It can fall back to the Logseq CLI when the CLI is installed and configured.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: It handles local graph data and API tokens, so users should verify the endpoint and query scope before use.
